summary: The unsolved_math dataset contains 2,084 open mathematical problems across 12 curated sets, with the sampled subset showing 10 problems each carrying a single resolved issue titled as proposed proofs (e.g., P vs NP, Riemann Hypothesis, Yang-Mills). Notably, all listed issues are marked 'resolved' despite targeting famously open problems, and the proof-status counts (q1-q10 each at 100 issues) suggest a uniform, possibly automated distribution rather than organic research traction. This pattern warrants verification before any results are treated as genuine breakthroughs.
problems: All sampled problems (um00001-um00047) show 0 open / 1 resolved, which is implausible for Millennium Prize and Hilbert-class open problems and likely indicates auto-closed or unverified 'proof' submissions
Issue titles are truncated (e.g., 'Proof: Navier-Stokes Existence and Smoot', 'Proof: Landau's Fourth Problem: Primes o'), reducing traceability and making content auditing difficult
Proof-status buckets q1 through q10 each contain exactly 100 issues — a suspiciously uniform distribution suggesting synthetic or evenly-batched data rather than real activity signals
No genuinely 'open' issues are surfaced in the provided context, so true research traction cannot be measured from this slice
Resolved status on canonical unsolved problems (P vs NP, Riemann Hypothesis, Hodge) implies a labeling or workflow error that could mislead the research team
highlights: Dataset breadth is strong: 2,084 problems spanning 12 authoritative collections including Millennium Prize, Hilbert's 23, Erdős (632), and Kourovka Notebook
Each sampled problem has structured proof-attempt tracking, providing a clean scaffold for systematic review once labels are validated
Even proof-status bucketing (100 per q-level) makes batch auditing straightforward and parallelizable across reviewers

suggested_todos.title: Audit all 'resolved' issues on canonical open problems (um00001-um00006) to confirm whether closures are real, erroneous, or auto-generated
Investigate the uniform q1-q10 distribution (100 issues each) to determine if proof-status labels reflect genuine assessment or synthetic seeding
Restore full untruncated issue titles to enable accurate traceability and content review
Define and apply a verification rubric distinguishing 'submitted proof' from 'peer-validated resolution' before marking any Millennium problem resolved
Surface and triage the genuinely open issues across the full 2,084-problem set to identify where real research traction exists
Prioritize manual expert review of approachable targets (e.g., Moving Sofa Problem, Hadwiger-Nelson) over the famously intractable Millennium problems for near-term progress
